title: TBM 391: You’re Not Misaligned. You’re Thinking Differently.
questions: Why do my teammates disagree even when we share the same intent and desired outcomes?
How can I tell whether the conflict is about thinking style (abstract vs concrete) rather than strategy or trust?
What should a facilitator do when participants need artifacts but others are still in exploratory mode?
How do we collaborate effectively when people have different windows for focus and different needs for examples?
search_terms: how to communicate with concrete thinkers
how to communicate with abstract thinkers
are we misaligned or just thinking differently
summary: The post argues that many workplace conflicts labeled as misalignment are actually clashes between abstract (purpose/pattern) and concrete (steps/artifacts) thinking styles. It’s aimed at leaders, facilitators, and product/strategy practitioners who need to collaborate effectively under uncertainty and stress. The core takeaway is to treat communication as “different cognitive languages” rather than a battle of right vs wrong.

title: TBM 385: "We Have To Simplify!"
questions: When someone says 'we need to simplify,' what do they actually mean in terms of priorities, processes, or structure?
How do you reduce bureaucracy and reporting without losing necessary governance?
What practical steps reduce silos and dependencies so teams can work with less coordination overhead?
How should AI be used in simplification efforts—what should it complement versus replace?
search_terms: AI for productivity vs process change
adapt practices to your environment not copy
summary: The post explores how the phrase 'we have to simplify' can mask multiple, conflicting interpretations—ranging from reducing priorities to cutting bureaucracy, silos, paperwork, and cognitive load. It is aimed at leaders and operators trying to turn vague calls for simplification into concrete operating-model changes, while cautioning against relying on AI or copying other companies without clarifying the real constraint.

title: TBM 321: "Reducing Complexity"
questions: What does “too complex” really mean in product and leadership contexts?
How can leaders simplify execution without trying to eliminate the inherent complexity of product work?
How do competing priorities and organizational structure increase cognitive load for teams?
What operating practices create bounded complexity within teams and simple interfaces across teams?
search_terms: leadership focus on conditions for complex work
summary: This post argues that executives should not try to “reduce complexity” in the work itself because product work is inherently complex and companies behave like complex adaptive systems. Instead, it reframes complexity complaints as high cognitive load driven by competing priorities, coordination drag, and unclear boundaries, and it advocates leadership actions that reduce friction while enabling teams to handle complexity internally. It’s aimed at product leaders and managers designing prioritization and collaboration practices.

title: TBM 313: Chat, Breathe, and Reflect
questions: How should a team change its communication patterns in a dynamic, uncertain environment?
What does it mean to reduce communication distance and increase information exchange frequency in practice?
Why do communication improvements fail when trust, psychological safety, or bandwidth are lacking?
How can an IC or non-senior team member increase sensemaking without self-gaslighting?
summary: The post argues that in dynamic, uncertain environments, effective teams must rewire communication by shortening information paths, increasing how often they exchange updates, and learning to interpret confounding inputs. It emphasizes that these changes require trust, psychological safety, cognitive flexibility, and sufficient bandwidth—otherwise they feel inefficient and can fail. It is aimed at leaders and individual contributors trying to navigate messy, high-ambiguity conditions (e.g., in tech) without losing coherence or morale.

title: TBM 242: The Simplicity Fetish
questions: When does my effort to simplify or summarize actually help alignment, and when does it create damage?
What does a flood of follow-up questions after a meeting reveal about missing dependencies and communication channels?
How can we design interfaces and feedback loops that support complex problem-solving without oversimplifying it?
Do we need more facilitation and shared understanding (e.g., mini-offsites) rather than more compressed messaging?
summary: The post argues that many workplaces suffer from “simplicity fetishism,” where leaders and systems try to boil complex, dynamic problems into transactional summaries. It’s aimed at managers and team leaders who want to improve alignment and communication by recognizing impedance mismatches, information overload, and the need for better interfaces and feedback loops for complex problem-solving.

title: 45A/52: Simplicity Is a Tactic, Not a Goal
questions: How do I know whether simplifying content will actually help my target audience?
Why do usability tests pass internally but fail with external users?
What does “simplicity” mean in my context—accessibility, memorability, or actionable clarity?
How can I prevent internal communications from being unintentionally vague or confusing?
search_terms: reduce cognitive load without losing precision
summary: The post argues that simplicity should be treated as a means to an explicit communication or product goal, not as a universal objective. It uses examples from aviation, usability testing, and internal messaging to show how “simple” can mean different things and how audience needs determine whether simplification helps or harms. It’s aimed at teams designing content, interfaces, or communications who want clarity without accidental vagueness.

title: TBM 34/52: Words Matter—Strategy & Certainty
questions: How do I map my strategy’s underlying assumptions by certainty, specificity, and competitor consensus?
When should decreasing certainty trigger new or emergent strategic assumptions?
Why do probabilistic words create confusion when communicating strategy?
How can we communicate uncertainty coherently without manufacturing false certainty or waiting for 100% certainty?
search_terms: we believe highly unlikely probably not
summary: The post argues that strategy should be treated as a dynamic system of assumptions with varying certainty, direction, specificity, and asymmetric impact. It is aimed at product and strategy leaders who struggle to communicate uncertainty clearly, highlighting how probabilistic words are perceived differently by audiences. The guidance is to revisit assumptions and apply disciplined, word-by-word probabilistic language (or confidence intervals) to avoid strategy whiplash and false precision.

title: 33/52: "That Isn't Focus!"
questions: Why does a strategy that feels focused to me seem vague to someone else?
How much detail should a strategy include for a given audience and team experience level?
When using coarse-grained direction under uncertainty, how do we communicate certainty and next steps?
Does an open-ended strategy empower the team or signal leadership negligence?
summary: The post explains why “focus” in strategy can be perceived differently across people and teams, especially when experience, domain knowledge, and information needs vary. It offers guidance for communicating strategy more actionably by clarifying assumptions, the level of certainty, and how much detail is appropriate for the target audience. It is aimed at leaders and teams trying to align on direction and decision-making under uncertainty.

title: 32/52: Telephone Game!
questions: How does information get distorted as it passes through multiple people in an organization?
Why do priorities skew (e.g., important becomes code red) as messages propagate?
What happens when questions and feedback flow back upstream?
How do feedback avoidance and information forking create ambiguity for managers?
summary: The post uses “telephone game” diagrams as a metaphor for how messages, priorities, and context mutate as they travel through teams. It’s aimed at people trying to improve organizational alignment, decision quality, and feedback practices by understanding how distortion, prescriptiveness, and avoidance behaviors compound over multiple hops.

title: TBM 28/52: First Focus. Then Simplify
questions: How do I tell the difference between focus and simplification when working on complex problems?
Why do teams make bad decisions when they approach complex challenges as simple ones?
What should a good strategy diagnosis do, and how is it different from making things “simple and memorable”?
When reducing many customer personas to a few, is it better to pick a subset or synthesize new personas?
search_terms: strategy diagnosis guiding policy coherent actions
summary: The post argues that complex problems should not be treated as simple, even if “simplifying” feels like reducing cognitive load. It’s aimed at leaders and teams who struggle to align on strategy, messaging, and customer personas, and need a practical way to diagnose first and then run focused experiments. It also highlights the common failure mode where teams confuse simple communication with true focus and context awareness.

title: TBM 27b/52: Why Most Strategies Lack Clarity
questions: Why do so many product strategies feel vague or incoherent?
Is it possible to have a clear strategy without being certain?
How should leaders communicate uncertainty to stakeholders who demand certainty?
What causes leaders to hedge or obfuscate strategy messaging?
search_terms: Miro mandate levels competitive analysis can should templates
summary: The post argues that many product strategies lack clarity because leaders equate coherence with certainty and then avoid uncertainty due to fear and stakeholder pressure. It’s aimed at product and strategy leaders who need a way to communicate a coherent direction while explicitly acknowledging what is known, assumed, and believed.

title: TBM 30/52: What the **** Does That Even Mean?
questions: Which words in our team’s communication cause the most confusion?
Where should we focus our conversations and energy to reduce misunderstanding?
How do we decide whether a term needs shared definition, can tolerate disagreement, or should be left undefined?
What should we do with language that isn’t helpful or is actively harmful?
summary: This post offers a lightweight facilitation activity for teams to surface confusing terminology and decide how to handle it. It’s aimed at leaders and facilitators who want practical ways to align on definitions, tolerate productive disagreement, and identify words to leave undefined or stop using.
